data.title: In farm bill, GOP takes another swing at pesticide protections loathed by MAHA
data.description:
Republicans are using the farm bill to take another swing at passing legislation to protect pesticides, something sure to anger their allies in the Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) movement.

A House Republican iteration of the farm bill released on Friday includes a provision that prevents states and courts from penalizing pesticide makers for failing to include warnings on their label about health effects that go beyond those formally recognized by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).

Proponents of such measures say they prevent companies from having to comply with a hodgepodge of rules that could differ from place-to-place.

However, opponents of such language argue that it could be used to tamp down on failure-to-warn lawsuits that seek to hold pesticide companies accountable for not disclosing alleged harms of their products.

Similar language was ultimately removed from an EPA appropriations package earlier this year amid pushback from Democrats and MAHA-aligned Republicans.

The MAHA movement has been spearheaded by Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. Its members are largely skeptical of big business, including Big Pharma, Big Ag and Big Chemical — breaking with other Republicans who are often big business allies.

While the measure applies to pesticides broadly, the issue is often talked about in terms of glyphosate, which has been a key ingredient in the weedkiller Roundup.

While the EPA says there’s “insufficient evidence to conclude that glyphosate plays a role in any human diseases,” multiple lawsuits have claimed otherwise — and have cost Bayer billions of dollars.

The question of whether such lawsuits are preempted under federal law will be reviewed by the Supreme Court this year.

data.title: Al Green slams crypto spending in Texas primary: 'We cannot allow the crypto industry to own Congress'
data.description:
Rep. Al Green (D-Texas) on Friday slammed a crypto super PAC’s plans to spend $1.5 million to oppose him, arguing that lawmakers “cannot allow the crypto industry to own Congress.” 

“They’re going to do whatever they can to maintain their dominance that they have with some members of Congress, but not with me,” Green said Friday in a video statement shared to Facebook who added he was “ready to take on the crypto industry.”

“Yes, they will outspend me, but they cannot buy the new 18th Congressional District. I assure you — it will not be bought, and it is not for sale,” he added. 

Protect Progress, a super PAC affiliated with the pro-crypto Fairshake network, announced this week that the group is planning to spend over a million dollars to oust Green from his reelection bid in Texas’ 18th Congressional District. 

“As a member of the Financial Services Committee, Representative Al Green has decided to try and stop American innovation in its tracks,” the group’s spokesman, Josh Vlasto, said in a statement. 

“Texas voters can no longer sit by and have representation in Congress that is actively hostile towards a growing Texas crypto community,” he continued. “We are committed to electing new members who embrace innovation, growth and wealth creation for all Americans.” 

Green is running against Rep. Christian Menefee (D-Texas) in the newly drawn House district. Menefee recently won a special election to fill the late Rep. Sylvester Turner’s (D-Texas) seat in the current 18th Congressional District.  

Polling released from the Hobby School of Public Affairs at the University of Houston on Friday showed Menefee leading in the Democratic primary with 52 percent to Green’s 28 percent.  

Another super PAC affiliated with Fairshake recently announced it would be spending $5 million to support Rep. Barry Moore (R-Ala.) in his Senate bid this year.  

Crypto spent heavily in the last cycle, primarily targeting former Rep. Katie Porter’s (D-Calif.) campaign for Senate and former Sen. Sherrod Brown’s (D-Ohio) reelection efforts. At the same time, the industry boosted Sen. Ruben Gallego (D-Ariz.) and Elissa Slotkin (D-Mich.) in their successful Senate bids, among others.  

Fairshake spent close to $180 million in the 2024 cycle; the group has well above that amount — over $193 million — in the bank to start of 2026.  

Green waved off polling showing him behind in the Democratic primary, saying the poll he would be following was the March 3 primary. 

He lashed out at the crypto industry, arguing the super PACs “want to intimidate other members of Congress.” 

“They want to spend this money on me so that they can cause others to quake in their boots, in some sense, but I believe other members are going to be encouraged by the fact that I’m going to win, and after I win, then they’ll have to rethink what they’re doing,” the Texas Democrat said.

“We cannot allow the crypto industry to own Congress,” Green added.

data.title: Trump on Nancy Guthrie investigation: 'Progress has been made'
data.description:
President Trump on Friday said the investigation into the disappearance of Nancy Guthrie was making “progress” as the search nears the two-week mark.

“[The FBI] took it over originally. It was a local case originally and they didn’t want to let go of it, which is fine,” Trump told reporters at the White House on Friday as he was departing for North Carolina.

When asked why the FBI was not the lead agency overseeing the investigation, Trump responded: “It’s up to them, it’s really up to the community but ultimately where the FBI got involved, I think, you know, progress has been made.”

Trump was also asked if he believed cartels might be involved in Guthrie’s disappearance but said it was too soon to make such a determination.

“Somebody either knew what they were doing very well or they were ranked amateurs. Either way, it’s not a good situation,” the president said.

Guthrie has been missing for the past 12 days. In the latest update on her disappearance, investigators located a pair of black gloves near her home in Tuscon, Arizona, which they will run through a DNA analysis. 

The Pima County Sheriff’s Department has said that close to 18,000 calls have come in to report information through the tip line for Guthrie’s case. TMZ founder Harvey Levin said the tabloid news organization received a letter from someone claiming to know the kidnapper and that they had tried to reach Guthrie’s siblings without success. 

The letter demanded one Bitcoin sent to an address that TMZ confirmed was active.

The FBI said Wednesday that they were sweeping the roadways along Arizona’s Catalina Foothills looking for Guthrie. The Phoenix field office said they were conducting an “extensive search” of the area as well.

The FBI released footage of an armed man who was wearing a mask outside Guthrie’s home from the same night that she disappeared. The agency has said that they are investigating “persons of interest” but have not named any suspects in the case yet.

Savannah Guthrie posted the video of the kidnapper outside her mother’s home on Instagram Wednesday with the caption, “Someone out there recognizes this person. We believe she is still out there. Bring her home.” 

Officials doubled a reward for information regarding the kidnapper to $100,000 Friday.

Rema Rahman contributed.

data.title: Trump says he's talking to Democrats about DHS: 'We'll see what happens'
data.description:
President Trump on Friday said that talks are moving forward with Senate Democrats over a deal to fund the Department of Homeland Security, which is set to shut down at midnight after the upper chamber left Washington without a deal. 

“We’ll see what happens,” Trump told reporters as he left the White House for Fort Bragg.

The two sides have held negotiations for roughly a week, with talks expected to continue through the weekend. 

Democrats demanding reforms to Immigration and Customs Enforcement have centered the negotiations on tightening of warrant requirements, unmasking agents who are engaged in immigration operations and ending roving patrols in cities and communities. All have appeared to be bright red lines for Republicans, with few showing signs of being willing to yield on the three issues.

Democrats rejected the White House’s latest offer on Thursday. Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) labeled it “not serious, plain and simple.”

Trump added that he knows “what [Democrats] want” and what they can live with, but declined to offer details. 

“You always have to protect our law enforcement. They’ve done a great job. Remember, they’ve taken out hundreds of thousands of criminals out of our country,” he said. “We have to protect our law enforcement. … [Immigrations and Customs Enforcement] has done a phenomenal job.”

“We have … a closed border for the first time in history,” Trump continued. “I know what they want. I know what they can live with. The Democrats have gone crazy. They’re radical left lunatics. That’s why their cities are so unsafe.The Blue cities are the cities that are unsafe.”

GOP and Democratic appropriators had a full-year deal to fund DHS prior to the shooting of Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, which spurred the minority party to revolt against the deal.

Rema Rahman contributed.

data.title: Could the next AOC emerge from San Francisco?
data.description:
For the first time since 1987, San Francisco’s congressional seat is up for grabs. With Rep. Nancy Pelosi (D-Calif.) stepping aside, the race ahead of the June 2 Democratic primary poses a familiar question: Should this be a routine succession or an insurgent upset?

In 2018, Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.), then a little-known organizer, defeated 10-term Democratic Rep. Joe Crowley (D-N.Y.) in New York’s 14th District. Now, we may see a similar victory in San Francisco: A younger, tech-savvy progressive, very much in AOC’s mold, is challenging a party stalwart and calling for a new era of Democratic leadership.

The establishment candidate is state Sen. Scott Wiener (D), a mainstream California Democrat seen as the heir apparent to Pelosi. The insurgent is Saikat Chakrabarti (pronounced “shoi-cut” like “boycott”), a founding engineer at Stripe who cashed out after the company became a multibillion-dollar tech giant. He went on subsequently to become a left-wing political organizer.

Chakrabarti co-founded Justice Democrats, the organization behind progressive primary challengers like Ilhan Omar and Cori Bush, and ran Ocasio-Cortez’s congressional campaign before serving as her chief of staff. Chakrabarti and Ocasio-Cortez are buddy-buddy — he calls her “Alex.” Both worked on the 2016 presidential campaign of Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.).

Chakrabarti launched his campaign long before Pelosi announced her retirement, framing his message as a challenge to both President Trump’s authoritarianism and what he calls a complacent Democratic establishment. “We are in this era where we need new ideas, we need new leaders, we need people who are going to push the party in a new direction,” he said. Chakrabarti leans on aggressive grassroots organizing — town halls, phone banks and door-knocking — tried-and-true methods for outsiders such as Zohran Mamdani, whose campaign mounted one of the largest canvassing operations in recent memory.

Last month, I volunteered on several of Chakrabarti’s canvasses. From my experience, San Franciscans seem broadly receptive to his associations with Ocasio-Cortez and Sanders, which we were explicitly encouraged to mention. Many volunteers I met had been knocking on doors since last spring.

Meanwhile, Wiener has held exactly one canvassing event. He entered the race after Pelosi announced her retirement, though he had been quietly fundraising through a committee for years — waiting his turn, in keeping with party norms that frown upon primary challenges. 

A prominent YIMBY or “yes in my back yard,” Wiener’s donor base includes developers and business groups that have long bankrolled his campaigns. This cycle, he raised $2.8 million — $730,000 the day after launching his bid — mostly in donations of $2,000 or more. His prior campaigns leaned heavily on PAC money, even by California’s notoriously expensive standards.

Chakrabarti’s fundraising is markedly different. Less than 20 percent of his donations are $2,000 or more, while about 60 percent come from donors giving under $200. He reports more than twice as many individual donors as Wiener. So far, he’s raised about $300,000.

The significant distinction between Chakrabarti and prior progressive insurgents, however, is personal fortune. After his success at Stripe, Chakrabarti became a multimillionaire and is self-funding much of his campaign. He’s already loaned it $1.47 million, prompting Wiener to accuse him of running an “entirely astroturf[ed] campaign” with “no local support.” 

Chakrabarti, for his part, has turned the tables by being unapologetic about his wealth. “I am calling for a tax on myself. The winner-take-all economy is a big reason I got into politics,” he said. 

Chakrabarti argues that PACs can’t buy him like they can buy Wiener — he already has money. This positions him as a “class traitor,” a wealthy tech executive who wants to raise his own taxes.

That candid posture works for insurgent candidates. Ocasio-Cortez, Mamdani and the like all exude sheer bluntness about who they were and what they believed. Even Trump relied on a “class traitor” angle during his first presidential run. He was the outsider with bold opinions, beholden to no one. 

It’s hard to imagine San Francisco voters objecting to that pitch; they elected Daniel Lurie, a billionaire mayor who poured $8 million of his own money into the campaign.

Wiener, meanwhile, faces the vulnerabilities that come with establishment status. An avowed Zionist and co-chair of the state Legislative Jewish Caucus, Wiener was jeered during a debate when he refused to say whether Israel committed genocide in Gaza, then gave in immediately to public blowback and labeled it a genocide the next day.

What voters want more than ideological sparring is a candidate who can’t be bought and who champions causes that benefit San Franciscans — whether that leads them to the centrist Mayor Lurie, or to a progressive like Chakrabarti.

Alternatively, the district could elect Wiener, another foot soldier within the Democratic establishment — someone who will carouse for a while but then join the league of old guards, as Pelosi did.

California has sent many such figures to Capitol Hill, who often amass power and serve the state well. The question is whether that model has outlived its usefulness.

William Liang is a writer living in the San Francisco Bay Area.

data.title: Trump: Failed Iran talks would be a 'bad day' for Tehran
data.description:
President Trump on Friday said he is hopeful U.S. negotiations with Iran on its nuclear program will be successful, but warned that if they fail, it would be a “bad day” for Tehran.

As the U.S. builds up its defense forces in the Middle East, the Trump administration is also negotiating with the Islamic Republican about its nuclear program. Trump reiterated his preference for letting the talks continue but also offered a stern warning when asked if he believed the talks would succeed.

“I think they’ll be successful. And if they’re not, it’s going to be a bad day for Iran, very bad,” Trump told reporters at the White House ahead of his trip to Fort Bragg to honor military members involved in the Venezuela operation that captured ex-leader Nicolás Maduro.

Trump said a second aircraft carrier he ordered to the Middle East would be leaving soon in the event it would be needed should talks collapse.

“We have one out there that just arrived. If we need it, we’ll, well, we have it ready,” he said.  “A big, a very big force.”

Trump has threatened Iranian leaders over their brutal treatment of anti-regime protesters in which thousands of people have been killed since the uprising began late last year. But talks over whether Iran would come to a nuclear agreement have so far staved off attacks by the U.S. 

The president has also offered a blunt warning to Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, telling NBC News the Islamic leader should be “very worried.”

Trump met earlier this week with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to discuss the U.S. negotiations with Tehran, but the president said later “nothing definitive” was reached between the two leaders about how to continue.

Still, he said he made it clear to Netanyahu that his preference is to let talks persist.

“There was nothing definitive reached other than I insisted that negotiations with Iran continue to see whether or not a Deal can be consummated,” Trump wrote in a Truth Social post. “If it can, I let the Prime Minister know that will be a preference.”

“Last time Iran decided that they were better off not making a Deal, and they were hit with Midnight Hammer — That did not work well for them. Hopefully this time they will be more reasonable and responsible,” he added.

data.title: Live updates: DHS shutdown inches closer; Trump visits with troops in North Carolina
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) is gearing up for a shutdown after Senate Democrats on Thursday blocked bills that would keep the lights on.

Senators left Washington without a deal, showing just how far apart Democrats and White House negotiators are on a potential agreement to reform Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) after two fatal shootings in Minneapolis heightened scrutiny of DHS.

Starting at midnight Saturday, key agencies overseen by the department — including the Transportation Security Administration, Federal Emergency Management Agency, U.S. Coast Guard and U.S. Secret Service, among others — will see a lapse in funding.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) told their colleagues to be prepared to return to the Capitol on short notice if an agreement is reached.

President Trump, ahead of his trip to North Carolina, told reporters that he is still open to negotiating with Democrats on the issue.

The president is visiting Fort Bragg on Friday to speak with military families and the specialized troops that assisted with the ouster of embattled Venezuelan leader Nicolás Maduro earlier this year.

Trump will then travel to his Mar-a-Lago resort in Palm Beach, Fla., for the weekend.

Follow along live below.

data.title: Former UK ambassador Mandelson summoned to testify before Congress over Epstein links
data.description:
A former UK ambassador has been summoned to testify before Congress over his ties to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. 

Two members of the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform requested testimony from Peter Mandelson in a Friday email to the former British official as part of the panel’s investigation into Epstein’s activities. 

Ranking Member Robert Garcia (D-Calif.) and Rep. Suhas Subramanyam (D-Va.) said that the Justice Department’s files related to its Epstein probe indicated that Mandelson “may have knowledge of his activities related to our investigation.”

“In the interest of justice for the victims of Jeffrey Epstein, we request that you cooperate with the Committee’s investigation by sitting for a transcribed interview with the Committee,” the lawmakers wrote.

Mandelson stepped down from his role as British ambassador to the U.S. after links to Epstein became public. He previously served as Britain’s member of the EU Commission and was a member of Parliament. 

Garcia and Subramanyam pointed to “evidence … demonstrating your close ties” to Epstein in their Friday letter, including a handwritten note in the sex offender’s 50th Birthday Book and financial transfers from Epstein. Garcia and Subramanyam also referenced an image included in the Epstein files that “appears to depict you in your underwear alongside an
unidentified woman.”

“While you no longer serve as British Ambassador to the United States and have stepped down from the House of Lords, it is clear that you possessed extensive social and business ties to Jeffrey Epstein and hold critical information pertaining to our investigation of Epstein’s operations,” the lawmakers wrote. “Given the appalling allegations regarding Epstein’s conduct, we request that you make yourself available for a transcribed interview with Committee staff regarding the crimes of Jeffrey Epstein and his co-conspirators.”

Garcia and Subramanyan mandated a response from Mandelson by Feb. 27.

The letter comes amid the Oversight Committee’s continued investigation into Epstein. The committee has subpoenaed former President Bill Clinton and former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton to provide testimony in the investigation. The Clintons are set to appear before the committee at the end of February.

data.title: At 30, E-Rate is a proven success. Let's keep improving it.
data.description:
Thirty years ago, Congress passed the Telecommunications Act of 1996 based on a straightforward principle: The benefits of modern communications networks should reach every community, not just the well-resourced ones.

Back then, the “world wide web” was growing and increasingly necessary for daily life, but fewer than a quarter of U.S. adults had internet access. To address that gap efficiently, Congress created E-Rate, a discount matching program that enabled public libraries and schools across the country to obtain high-speed internet access at significantly reduced cost.

As the program hits its 30th anniversary this month, we should celebrate E-Rate for what it is: a quiet powerhouse that has given nearly every American access to high-speed internet through public libraries and schools.

For public libraries, the impact of E-Rate has a human face: a job seeker applying for a new position; workers filing taxes on time; a family accessing student homework portals, remote learning and telehealth appointments; and communities using library technology to keep in touch with loved ones during natural disasters. In many towns and neighborhoods, the public library remains the only reliable place to get broadband speeds that make modern life possible. When home connectivity is unavailable, unreliable or unaffordable, the library is where people come to get online.

The internet also powers the practical, behind-the-scenes functions that keep libraries running. The full range of library services requires high-speed internet: building and managing collections, checking materials in and out, supporting research, and helping patrons find credible information efficiently. Today’s card catalogs do not work without internet access.

Beyond powering countless library services, the E-Rate program provides broadband that entire communities rely on. Six tribal libraries and two schools in New Mexico, for example, leveraged their access to E-Rate funding and built two tribally owned and operated modern fiber networks. Covering 120 miles and six sovereign nations, the networks dramatically increased internet access speeds and decreased costs, and it would not have been built without federal E-Rate funding.

Not only has E-Rate made broadband more accessible for communities over its 30-year history, it is now considered a model for other federal programs in terms of oversight for federal awards and grants. Of five programs reviewed by the Government Accountability Office in 2025 for compliance with requirements for preventing waste, fraud and abuse, E-Rate was the only one to receive a perfect score.

Despite the success of E-Rate in filling connectivity gaps over the years, the program is not perfect. Only about half of public libraries participate in E-Rate. The complexity of the application process continues to be a key barrier. Many libraries, especially small, rural and tribal, operate with limited administrative capacity. When a single person is the director, technology lead and grant writer, complicated forms, procurement requirements and compliance rules can turn a valuable benefit into an unrealistic burden. For a large system with specialized staff, complexity is inconvenient. For a small library, it can be disqualifying.

Over the last 30 years, ALA has continually worked with the FCC to improve the program, remove barriers, and increase access to this vital program for library applicants. The FCC can simplify applications and procurement steps and preserve E-Rate’s already strong program integrity safeguards. These streamlining measures mean less time spent navigating paperwork and more time spent delivering public value, and more libraries across the country with highspeed broadband.

Congress, which has long recognized that E-Rate is as important today as it was in 1996, has its own part to play in ensuring the program remains a critical part of the Universal Service Fund. Today, a bipartisan, bicameral working group is considering reforms to ensure the Universal Service Fund, including the E-Rate discount, continues well into the future. As Congress moves toward contribution reform, they should prioritize reliable funding that is not dependent on annual appropriations.

Congress should also keep its eye on the courts. The Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of the Universal Service Fund in 2025, but recent renewed attacks claim that E-Rate specifically is unconstitutional. Threats to a program with such results — and fiscal integrity that exceeds many government programs — are short-sighted and jeopardize efforts to bridge the digital divide for the nearly one in ten Americans that have no high-speed internet access.

Anniversaries are a time to take stock. Thirty years after the Telecommunications Act of 1996, the country is more connected, but many people still rely on public institutions to reach the digital world. E-Rate is one of the most effective, accountable tools the nation has for making sure that geography and income do not determine who can learn, work and participate in our connected world. We should celebrate E-Rate and — until broadband is universally accessible and affordable — strengthen it.

Sam Helmick is president of the American Library Association and serves as community and access services coordinator for Iowa City Public Library.

data.title: Rhode Island State House rug that cost $70K gets state seal wrong
data.description:
PROVIDENCE, R.I. (WPRI) — Officials are blaming “human error” after a new rug at the Rhode Island State House somehow got the seal of approval despite having the wrong seal.

The $70,000 rug was installed Feb. 2 in the second-floor State Room, which is located next to the governor’s office and is often the setting for news conferences and ceremonial events, with Gilbert Stuart’s historic portrait of George Washington hanging on the wall.

But Nexstar’s WPRI discovered that the 1,125-square-foot floor covering has the wrong state seal stitched into it, not once but twice. Both bear the words: “Seal of Rhode Island and Providence.” Voters removed the phrase “and Providence Plantations” from the state’s official name in 2020.

The two inaccurate seals are currently being covered up by round area rugs, which still have their $110 price tags from Ocean State Job Lot on the bottom.

Karen Greco, a spokesperson for the R.I. Department of Administration, acknowledged the mishap in an email.

“The mistake on the seal was simply human error, and the plan is to correct it,” she told WPRI. She did not say what office was responsible.

It’s unclear how much more money taxpayers will have to spend fixing the seals. The rug was purchased from Stark Carpet Corp. of Boston.

“The two seals will be hand-cut out of the rug and new seals will be sewn in,” Greco said. “We are waiting for a timeline and a cost estimate for the correction.”

Stark Carpet has not responded to a request for comment.

Replacing the rug was one of many items state officials put on their to-do list after the 2020 vote to remove the “Plantations” phrase from the end of the state’s formal name, requiring a change to the state seal.

The McKee administration has been making slow progress since then, removing the language from various other locations at the State House, though some are harder to address than others.
